meta.note: Generated from the recorded demo via tools/sidekick-import; edit talk tracks in-app.

context.title: Discovery — Subcontractor Hub's situation (00:08–00:18)
facts: ~25 international employees paid directly today via international bank wires — per-wire fees on every payment (Nate).
Countries in play: Philippines, Mexico, South Africa (contractors today) + the Bulgaria team + 1 UK person paid to a US bank account.
Bulgaria team is currently a vendor: they invoice, individuals aren't paid directly.
A prior EOR-ish provider (~$20–25/person as contractors) was abandoned because it didn't integrate with TriNet.
Driver #1 (Justin, repeatedly): W2-style employment for Bulgaria — IP protection and stopping suspected payroll scraping (leadership told X, workers paid Y).
Priority order: Bulgaria EOR first; UK person also wanted as W2; other countries later.
Justin is 'hyper-focused' on Bulgaria being done right — decision-maker energy on this one thread.

context.title: EOR mechanics + rollout expectations (00:18–00:23)
facts: IP language is in the EOR employment contract; client can layer their own lawyer-reviewed IP agreement — Shane took that as an action item.
Employee onboarding is ~a two-week process (contracts, notifications, worker self-entry).
Staggered rollout (Bulgaria/international first) is possible; caveat: separate direct login until the rest lands.
Bulgaria team's current company is owned by its leaders (Peter, Alec) 'as far as we know' — notice-period risk considered low but unverified.

context.title: Pricing question (00:49–00:51)
facts: Justin asked cost directly; Shane: rates being finalized by country, 'not thousands — usually hundreds', quoted a $550–$700-ish PEPM range for EOR.
Consistent with the Tier-2 $550 SMB list price on the Bulgaria sheet; do NOT anchor below it.

context.title: Support model (00:56–00:58)
facts: Beyond the tech: named implementation manager plus human support for the employment itself (separations, compliance questions, country rules).
Positioning line worth keeping: 'It's more than payment — real people manage this employment for you.'

context.title: Wrap + committed next steps (00:58–01:01)
facts: Bryce owes: (1) presentable direct pricing — 'what are the economics for you guys'; (2) a deployment timeline.
Shane/Antaeus offered to field follow-ups on financials and contractual arrangement.
→ The Bulgaria pricing sheet we built is deliverable (1). Timeline doc is still open.

screens.title: Prism Global platform — home
screens.what: The admin landing view — the first screen a Subcontractor Hub admin sees: the global workforce at a glance, entry points to payments, hiring, and reports.
capabilities: Single home for the global workforce
Admin login for the client's team
Jumping-off point to payments, hiring, time off, reports
screens.say: This is the Prism Global platform — this is what all of your admins see when they log in. There's the platform piece you're looking at, and a whole human support layer that runs alongside it; I'll talk through both at the same time.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Confirm which landing view is shown (admin home vs. workforce overview).

screens.title: Pay period — all payment types in one view
navPath: Pay period — all payment types in one view
screens.what: The monthly pay-period review: every worker payment (fixed, hourly, one-time, reimbursement) in a single reviewable list before it's finalized.
screens.say: The pay period for the next month shows every payment to all your contractors — scroll down and you see additional payments, one-time payments, hourly workers, fixed-rate workers, reimbursements. However the contract is set up — fixed rate per month, per project, or hourly — it's all reflected here.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Verify whether this is the payments list or an invoice detail; note the visible payment-type rows.

screens.title: Invoice breakdown by country — gross to net, no wire fees
navPath: Invoice breakdown by country — gross to net, no wire fees
screens.what: The client invoice with per-country breakdown: employees grouped by country, wages, employer taxes, gross-to-net — one US-originated payment replacing per-employee international wires.
capabilities: One US payment — no international wire fees
de: Kills the per-wire fees Nate pays today on ~25 direct international payments
screens.say: All the local taxes are broken out here, by country. You see the employees in each country, click in, and see exactly what the wages are, gross to net. And there are no international wire fees — you pay our invoice in the US, we convert and pay their bank accounts directly, and we pay the Bulgarian authorities their taxes.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Confirm the by-country grouping and a per-employee drill-in are on screen.

screens.title: Time off — statutory leave honored per country
navPath: Time off — statutory leave honored per country
screens.what: The time-off surface: statutory vacation/holiday/leave entitlements per country baked in, with requests and balances tracked against them.
screens.say: Time-off requests live here. Bulgaria has a statutory amount of paid vacation, holidays, and leaves an employer must provide — we always honor the country-specific minimums. You can always offer more; we can never offer less than the local standard.

screens.title: Worker documents — the employment contract chain
navPath: Worker documents — the employment contract chain
screens.what: A worker's documents: the signed employment contract (employee ↔ PrismHR as EOR) and the client assignment agreement — where IP protection language lives.
capabilities: Signed contracts on the worker record
IP protection language in the employment contract
de: Directly answers Justin's IP concern — and they can layer their own lawyer-reviewed IP agreement on top
screens.say: Her signed employment contract lives here. That contract is between the employee and PrismHR, because we're the local employer of record — and our agreement with you assigns her to work on your behalf. IP protection sits in the employment contract with the employee directly.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Check whether a worker profile/documents screen is visible or this is spoken over another view.

screens.title: Contractor payments — wallet-funded, no statutory load
navPath: Contractor payments — wallet-funded, no statutory load
screens.what: Contractor payment handling (contracted amount, wallet-funded) alongside the Q&A on employee-side statutory costs per country.
capabilities: Country statutory costs itemized for employees
screens.say: For contractors, we manage the payment against the contracted amount — no statutory leave or benefits involved. And those payments aren't direct wires into each bank account: you pay into a wallet, and it flows from there. On employees, every country's social charges are itemized — none of that healthcare is free; it's paid through those charges, and country-specific extras (like Mexico's stipends) we'll spell out per country.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Confirm what's on screen during the benefits/social-charges Q&A (moments 29–30) — did the view change?

screens.title: Add a worker — employee (EOR) vs. contractor
navPath: Add a worker — employee (EOR) vs. contractor
screens.what: The fork at the start of the add-worker flow: contractor payments vs. full EOR employment, with misclassification risk analysis when a contractor looks like an employee.
screens.say: Two roads when you add someone: employee or contractor. Contractor is simply contractor payments — you own the relationship, we move the money. But if they're acting exactly like an employee, we'll run a misclassification analysis and show you the real risks — fines and penalties are real if the government finds out — and we'd urge employer-of-record. Ultimately it's your decision. For Bulgaria, we'll choose employee.

screens.title: Where they live is where they're employed
navPath: Where they live is where they're employed
screens.what: The residency / work-authorization step: country of employment is set by where the worker physically lives, independent of nationality.
capabilities: Country of employment driven by residence
Catches the Bulgaria-vs-Spain seat before contracts are cut
de: This is the live open item on the subhub deal — one worker possibly in Spain; confirm residency before the seat math is final
screens.say: Where they live is where they need to be employed — that's the rule the flow enforces. All twelve are Bulgarian nationals, but if one lives in Spain, that one is employed under Spain, not Bulgaria. Nationality and country of employment are separate questions, and this step is where it's settled.

screens.title: Role setup — title, description, start window
navPath: Role setup — title, description, start window
screens.what: The role-details step of the hire: job title, description, level — feeding the local employment contract, with the ~2-week onboarding window covering country-specific registrations.
capabilities: Country pre-start requirements handled in the 2-week window
screens.say: The rest are Bulgarian nationals in Bulgaria with work authorization, so setup is simple: title — project managers, mid-level — a quick description of the role, and the details. From there the employment contract gets worked on; some countries add registrations or waiting periods (France and the Philippines even require a doctor visit), which is what the roughly two-week window absorbs before they can start.

screens.title: Comp extras — recurring allowances + country PTO
navPath: Comp extras — recurring allowances + country PTO
screens.what: The compensation-extras step: recurring allowances per worker and the country's statutory PTO surfaced inline.
capabilities: Country statutory PTO surfaced at setup
screens.say: I can set up additional recurring payments right here — say a $100 monthly mobile allowance — and it lands in every paycheck going forward. Vacation and paid time off show exactly what Bulgaria allows, so you know the statutory floor while you decide what to offer.

screens.title: Equipment — third-party provisioning, not US shipping
navPath: Equipment — third-party provisioning, not US shipping
screens.what: The equipment step: provisioning laptops/monitors through an in-country third-party catalog instead of exporting hardware from the US.
capabilities: Avoids customs risk on US-shipped hardware
screens.say: Equipment policy: don't ship laptops from the US — they get stuck in customs and it's expensive. We have a third party you work with directly; it's a menu of IT equipment delivered to the worker's home or office in-country.

screens.title: Benefits — supplemental health at a flat PEPM
navPath: Benefits — supplemental health at a flat PEPM
screens.what: The optional benefits step: supplemental health at a flat per-employee-per-month rate with configurable cost-sharing.
capabilities: Flat-rate supplemental health ($210 PEPM as quoted)
Dependent coverage at the same rate
screens.say: Supplemental health is an extra $210 per employee per month if you choose it. You can absorb it, split it, or leave dependents to the employee at the same rate — and clicking in shows exactly what the plan covers.

screens.title: Offer letter — generated, client-reviewed, then sent
navPath: Offer letter — generated, client-reviewed, then sent
screens.what: The offer-letter step: implementation team generates it from the hire setup; the client approves before it goes to the worker.
capabilities: Auto-generated offer letter from hire data
Salary + benefits captured in one document
screens.say: Our team verifies everything and generates the offer letter — salary, benefits, everything you agreed. You review it before it ever reaches the employee, and any back-and-forth happens at the offer stage, not after.

screens.title: What the worker sees — payslips, time off, approvals
navPath: What the worker sees — payslips, time off, approvals
screens.what: The worker's self-service portal: payments history, payslips, leave balances, time-off requests with configurable approval chains.
screens.say: This is the employee's own view: every payment, their payslips, their holidays and leave balance — and requesting time off happens right here. Approvals route however you like: frontline manager first, HR second.

screens.title: Onboarding tracker — from offer letter to signed
navPath: Onboarding tracker — from offer letter to signed
screens.what: The onboarding status board: each in-flight hire with its stage (offer letter out → agreement signed → info collected).
screens.say: Down here — Laurent in Bulgaria, awaiting his offer letter. The implementation manager preps and sends it, and the row flips when the agreement's signed. Do this once and it's easy; the employee uploads their own information from there.

screens.title: Reports — workers and gross-to-net by country
navPath: Reports — workers and gross-to-net by country
screens.what: The reporting surface: workforce composition over time and per-country gross-to-net payroll detail.
capabilities: Worker report (contractor vs EOR mix, monthly)
screens.say: A couple of reports worth knowing: the worker report — month by month, how many contractors and EOR employees you have — and the gross-to-net report per country, showing exactly what you're paying and where it goes.

screens.title: Compensation calculator — true employer cost per country
navPath: Compensation calculator — true employer cost per country
screens.what: The tools library, led by the compensation calculator: salary in → employer all-in cost and employee net out, per country.
de: Directly powers the subhub all-in question — gross + employer burden + fee (the sheet we built)
screens.say: The compensation calculator shows the true cost of hiring in a country: enter the salary and it returns exactly what the employer pays all-in and what the employee takes home. Bulgaria's being added — we ran the UK and Australia live.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Bulgaria was missing from the calculator during the demo (Shane flagged it) — note which countries were shown (Australia, UK).

screens.title: The wallet — contractor payout without wires
navPath: The wallet — contractor payout without wires
screens.what: The contractor wallet: client funds it with one payment; workers withdraw to bank, hold USD stablecoin, or spend via digital card — replacing per-payment international wires on both sides.
capabilities: One client payment funds all payouts
USD stablecoin hold for unstable currencies
Flat PEPM regardless of payout count
branching: Applies to the contractor road from the add-worker fork (screen-07)
screens.say: Right now you're wiring money across the globe — expensive, and it signals payroll activity to those countries. Instead you pay us once; workers draw from a wallet: transfer to their bank for a $1–3 fee, hold in US-dollar stablecoin if their local currency is unstable, spin up a digital credit card, even crypto. For you it's a flat per-employee-per-month fee no matter how many payouts — and contractors stop losing $40–50 a withdrawal.
screens.needsFrameCheck: Wallet may deserve its own module (not in the current 13). Confirm which wallet screens are shown: balance, withdrawal, digital card, stablecoin.
